t: Comorbidity and exclusion of medical causes (frontal lobe injury, epilepsy, hypoglycemia).
In the context of an affective episode.
Persistent oppositional component with aggression, but outbursts are not primary.
— Coccaro E.F. et al. JAMA Psychiatry 2009 RCT — most evidence-based pharmacotherapy in IED; 20–60 mg/day.
CBT — anger management, cognitive restructuring, behavioral activation, relaxation. McCloskey M.S. et al. J Consult Clin Psychol 2008.
Mood stabilizers — valproate, lithium (off-label in refractory cases).
SSRI; evidence base from Coccaro RCT in IED.
— Trigger identification, cognitive restructuring, time-out, relaxation, problem-solving.
Significant improvement with fluoxetine and CBT.
Comorbid ASPD, substance use poor prognosis.
Clinical diagnosis based on frequency and functional impairment.
impulse control disorder — failure of control is clinical feature; evidence-based treatment (fluoxetine, CBT) required.
benzodiazepine paradoxical disinhibition can occur; contraindicated or used cautiously in IED.
legal punishment alone ineffective; clinical treatment required.
SSRI first-line; antipsychotic in refractory case.
Coccaro E.F. et al. A double-blind, randomized, placebo-controlled trial of fluoxetine in patients with intermittent explosive disorder. J Clin Psychiatry 2009;70(5):653–662.
McCloskey M.S. et al. Cognitive-behavioral therapy for intermittent explosive disorder. J Consult Clin Psychol 2008;76(5):876–886.
Kessler R.C. et al. The prevalence and correlates of DSM-IV intermittent explosive disorder. Arch Gen Psychiatry 2006;63(6):669–678.
